text: The patient is positioned in the jackknife position for elective surgery. A Lone Star retractor is placed to provide exposure, and Babcock forceps are used to avert the rectal prolapse.
The surgeon identifies the dentate line within the rectal mucosa, marking a key anatomical landmark during the procedure.
The surgeon measures the rectal prolapse and begins the incision two centimeters above the dentin line, using surgical instruments to carefully cut through the tissue.
During the elective surgery, the surgeon continues to dissect the rectum and mesorectum through a visible vascular plane, using precise techniques to separate these structures.
The surgeon coagulates and interrupts small mesorectal vessels near the rectal wall using electrocautery, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
In the emergency surgery, the surgeon encounters difficulty identifying the dental line due to venous congestion and edema, leading to bleeding during the sectioning of the rectum.
The surgeon uses single stitches and an ultrasound device to manage the tissue, avoiding the use of an electroscalpel.
The surgeon reaches the sigmoid colon and completes the circumferential section of the mesorectum, visually separating the mesorectal tissue from the surrounding structures.
In the emergency scenario, the surgeon lifts the sigmoid colon using a plastic loop to facilitate the surgical maneuver.
During elective surgery, the surgeon performs levatorplasty, addressing the diastasis of the pulmonary talus muscle branches.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler to divide the sigmoid colon, visibly accelerating the surgical procedure.

The surgeon performs a stitch-by-stitch colloanal anastomosis, carefully suturing the colon to the anal canal. Following the completion of the anastomosis, the retractor is removed from the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a laparoscopic diverting colostomy, carefully manipulating the altered tissue involved in the anastomosis to mitigate the high risk of leakage.
